name: Swift Ownership — Law of Exclusivity and Noncopyable Types
summary: Swift adds compile-time + runtime exclusivity enforcement on top of an ARC-managed language, then layers opt-in noncopyable types that get Rust-like static guarantees. SE-0176 Law of Exclusivity (Swift 4); SE-0390 noncopyable structs/enums (Swift 5.9, 2023); SE-0427 generics extension (Swift 6.0, 2024); SE-0377 consuming/borrowing parameter modifiers. Swift's clearest move into the Rust-adjacent design space — opt-in linearity layered on an ARC-managed language.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Swift adds compile-time + runtime exclusivity enforcement on top of an ARC-managed language, then layers opt-in noncopyable types that get Rust-like static guarantees. This is Swift's clearest move into the Rust-adjacent design space — opt-in linearity layered on an ARC-managed language.

sections.title: Law of Exclusivity (SE-0176)
sections.content:
The **Law of Exclusivity** (SE-0176, Swift 4): "two accesses to the same variable may not overlap unless both are reads" — enforced statically for locals/`inout`, dynamically (via per-variable access flags) for class properties and globals.

This is the foundation that makes `inout` parameters safe: `inout` is "copy-in / copy-out" semantically but compiles to in-place mutation under the exclusivity guarantee.

sections.title: Noncopyable structs and enums (SE-0390, SE-0427)
sections.content:
**SE-0390 noncopyable structs/enums** (Swift 5.9, 2023): `struct Foo: ~Copyable` suppresses the implicit `Copyable` conformance; values have unique ownership and may declare a `deinit`.

**SE-0427** (Swift 6.0, 2024) extends noncopyable types to generics via a default `Copyable` constraint that can be suppressed with `~Copyable`. This makes the linear-typed slice of Swift compose with the rest of the standard library — generic algorithms can be written once for both copyable and noncopyable inputs.

sections.title: Consuming / borrowing parameter modifiers (SE-0377)
sections.content: **SE-0377 parameter modifiers** make `consuming`/`borrowing` part of the API contract; `consuming` methods can use `discard self` to suppress `deinit`. Together with noncopyable types, this gives Swift a Rust-adjacent ownership API: consumers explicitly take ownership, borrowers explicitly do not, and the compiler enforces the discipline at the call boundary.
